what_it_does: This file documents a design option for integrating Obsidian with a board runtime: Obsidian edits are treated as events, validated by a watcher, and re-canonicalized through the runtime so the runtime remains the sole writer of canonical records.
observations: Option B is labeled the strong candidate among integration designs.
Obsidian may write, but its writes are events/proposals, not canonical truth.
A watcher or ingest operation re-renders changes through the board runtime and appends history.
Append-only writes (annotations, comments, replies, marks) are described as conflict-free because they create new addressed items rather than mutating existing files.
In-place writes (edit_item, transition) use last-writer-wins with the runtime as authority and append a history entry.
The design preserves truth, enables full Obsidian feature set including iOS write-back, and keeps the no-versioning law by appending history in the same file.
The watcher must re-serialize through the project's serializer and avoid treating YAML key reordering as a real edit.
The ingest path is identified as the lynchpin of correctness.
standouts: Runtime is the only canonical minter of records; Obsidian only suggests changes.
Append-only writes are framed as naturally conflict-free.
iOS write-back is enabled by treating every Obsidian edit as an ingest event.
concerns: Correctness depends on a watcher/ingest path that is described but not implemented here.
It assumes truly simultaneous in-place edits are rare rather than defining a robust merge strategy.
The watcher must reliably distinguish meaningful body edits from cosmetic YAML key reordering, which is a subtle correctness requirement.
notes: This item recommends a runtime-reconciled, two-way Obsidian integration that keeps the runtime as canonical authority while allowing Obsidian edits to flow back as validated events.
questions: What is the exact ingest protocol or event schema the watcher emits?
How does the watcher distinguish Obsidian body edits from YAML key reordering in practice?
How are offline or out-of-order Obsidian edits ordered when timestamps are close or clocks differ?
What does mark-is-truth refer to in this context?
purpose_vs_actual: The stated purpose is to present and justify Option B as the strong candidate, and the file does exactly that.
intention_signals: It is trying to justify selecting a two-way Obsidian integration that lets users edit from Obsidian/mobile without corrupting the canonical board records, by routing every external write through the board runtime for validation and re-serialization.
novelty: It treats an external editor's writes as proposals/events to be validated and re-canonicalized by the runtime rather than merging file changes directly or letting Obsidian co-own the canonical format.
contribution: It contributes a concrete reconciliation strategy and conflict model for co-authoring between Obsidian and the board runtime, preserving runtime authority while enabling mobile write-back and recording all edits as appended history.
summary_for_embedding: Noticeboard item item-89edf449 proposes Option B, a two-way Obsidian integration where Obsidian edits are ingested as events and reconciled by the board runtime, which remains the sole canonical writer.
intention_for_embedding: It was written to recommend a runtime-as-reconciler design that enables full Obsidian write-back while preserving canonical truth and minimizing write conflicts.
